title: Arithmetic regularity, removal, and progressions
speaker: Jacob Fox
abstract: A celebrated theorem of Roth from 1953 shows that every dense set of integers contains a three-term arithmetic progression. This has been the starting point for the development of an enormous amount of beautiful mathematics. In this talk, I will discuss some shocking developments over the last few months on the bounds on some of the well-studied variants of Roth's theorem.

proof_stages.label: Extremal progression problems and cap-set framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk situates Roth-type upper bounds, Behrend-type lower bounds, the cap-set problem in F_3^n, and the multicolored sum-free formulation as the main finite-field progression questions to be attacked.

proof_stages.label: Slice-rank proof of multicolored sum-free bounds
proof_stages.summary: Using Tao's slice-rank presentation, diagonal tensors and a monomial-counting argument give the Ellenberg--Gijswijt/Croot--Lev--Pach exponential bound and its multicolored sum-free extension.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic regularity and classical arithmetic triangle removal
proof_stages.summary: Green's arithmetic regularity lemma is introduced, applied to the arithmetic triangle removal lemma, and compared with the graph-triangle-removal reduction that yields tower-type bounds.

proof_stages.label: Tight polynomial arithmetic triangle-removal theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the sharp polynomial bound delta roughly epsilon^{C_p} and reduces the problem to a cleaned-up configuration of many disjoint rainbow triangles with roughly uniform local triangle counts.

proof_stages.label: Random affine-subspace reduction to multicolored sum-free
proof_stages.summary: A random affine subspace is sampled so that many surviving rainbow triangles become good and form a multicolored sum-free instance, from which the sharp arithmetic triangle-removal exponent follows.

proof_stages.label: Popular-difference Roth theorem and tower bounds
proof_stages.summary: Green's theorem guaranteeing a common difference with near-random progression density is sharpened by showing, with Pham and in related work with Zhao, that the necessary dimension bounds are tower-type of height about log(1/epsilon), with variants for weaker density targets and other groups.

proof_stages.label: Higher-dimensional affine subspaces and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The final stage derives upper bounds for forcing d-dimensional affine subspaces by iterating one-dimensional supersaturation, explains why popular-difference bounds are insufficient for the desired constants, and lists open problems on cap sets, longer progressions, other groups, and higher algebraic methods.

examples.name: The card game Set and a “super set”
examples.note: The Set deck is identified with F_3^4 and ordinary sets with three-term progressions, and the later nine-card “super set” found by the speaker's son is presented as the source of the higher-dimensional affine-subspace question.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker counts the 3^4=81 Set cards, models them as points of F_3^4, identifies sets/super-sets with affine lines/subspaces, and uses this to motivate bounds for higher-dimensional affine subspaces

passes.reason: The Set deck is modeled by F_3^4 and the “super set” by a regular 2-dimensional affine subspace, used as motivating structure rather than refutation.

examples.name: Fast matrix multiplication motivation
examples.note: The speaker recalls the n^3 matrix-multiplication algorithm and the Cohen--Umans group-theoretic approach to explain why multicolored sum-free bounds matter beyond additive combinatorics.

examples.note: In response to a question, a random set is used to illustrate that large subspaces should be epsilon-regular by Chernoff bounds and a union bound.

examples.name: Random density-alpha set for popular differences
examples.note: A random subset of F_3^n of density alpha is used as the benchmark explaining why alpha^3 is the natural target density for three-term progressions with a fixed common difference.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the random benchmark by noting that a fixed 3-term progression lies in a density-alpha random set with probability alpha^3 and compares popular differences to that value

passes.reason: A random density-alpha set attains the alpha^3 popular-difference benchmark up to concentration, showing tightness rather than serving as a counterexample.
A random density-alpha subset gives the typical alpha^3 popular-difference benchmark and tightness intuition, rather than serving as a counterexample.
